about.headline: A Tale of Swords and Music
about.body: Since 1883, HGD Sveta Cecilija has stood as the guardian of Korčula's most treasured ritual. While the fierce cadence of the Moreška sword dance once echoed across the entire Mediterranean, today it is performed here only, on the island of Korčula, by the sons of Korčula. Iron swords, archaic verse, and seven battles that have not changed in centuries. Come witness it.

schedule.headline: 2026 Season Performances
schedule.priceNote: Adults €20 · Children under 14 €10 · Groups: every 5th ticket free

history.headline: A Curated History
history.subline: Four moments that brought a Spanish ritual of mock combat to a Korčulan stone courtyard, and kept it there.

vignettes.title: The Dance Is Born
vignettes.body: The earliest recorded Moreška-style performance takes place in Lérida, commemorating the Christian capture of Aragon. A ritual of mock combat between two armies, one that will travel the Mediterranean for centuries before finding its final home.

vignettes.title: First Record in Korčula
vignettes.body: A town journal entry dated 7 March 1666 records a Moreška performance during carnival. The dance has already taken root. While similar traditions fade across Dalmatia, Korčula holds on, and never lets go.

vignettes.title: HGD Sveta Cecilija Is Founded
vignettes.body: Local citizens establish the Korčula Singing Society St. Cecily. Its founding statutes declare a mission to "ennoble the hearts of local youth" and defend Croatian language, song, and identity. The institutional home of Moreška is born.

vignettes.title: A New Era
vignettes.body: Following Croatia's declaration of independence, HGD Sveta Cecilija is officially re-established under its original name and mission. The society returns to its roots: preserving the Moreška and cultivating every dimension of Korčulan cultural life.

footer.tagline:
Guardians of the Steel Dance
since 1883.

aboutPage.heroHeadline: The Story of HGD Sveta Cecilija
aboutPage.heroSubtitle: Guardians of Korčula's living heritage since 1883
intro: Two kings. One captive maiden. A stolen love, a ritual challenge, and seven battles to decide the fate of honour. This is the Moreška, Europe's last surviving war dance, and the soul of Korčula. For over four centuries, men on this small Dalmatian island have drawn iron swords to re-enact a drama of virtue and force. The Bula, fiancée of the White King Osman, is held by Moro, the Black King who would claim her by force. Her reply cuts through the stone silence: 'Far more pain for me is your unwanted love than the steel.' What follows is the Sfida, the challenge, and then seven escalating figures of double-sword combat that have not changed in centuries.

What separates the Moreška from every other folk performance in Europe is the double-sword technique. Each moreškant wields two iron blades: the right hand strikes, the left parries, preserving the martial technique of a 16th-century Adriatic garrison. The swords weigh a kilogram each and strike hard enough to produce sparks. In 1937, Croatian composer Krsto Odak wrote the definitive brass band score that drives every figure: seven movements beginning in lyrical tension and escalating to a furious 4/4 finale as the Black army's circle tightens and yields. The costumes trace to 17th-century Italian court ballet: full pleated skirts, Roman cuffs, sashes. The White army wears red; the Black army wears black. There is nothing like it anywhere in Europe.

In 1883, during the Croatian National Revival, Korčulan citizens founded the Singing Society St. Cecily, today's HGD Sveta Cecilija. Their mission: to ennoble the hearts of local youth, defend the Croatian language, and keep the Moreška alive. What followed was a century and a half of institutional resilience. The society survived the Austro-Hungarian empire and two World Wars. The second destroyed the costumes, the swords, and the scores entirely. As the island was liberated in 1944, town barber Ivo Lozica and schoolteacher Božo Jeričević trained a new troop of boys aged 12 to 17 from scratch, sewing costumes from church altar cloth. In 1991, HGD Sveta Cecilija was re-established under its original name following Croatian independence. The dance had survived everything.

In Korčula, there is a saying: 'You are not a male if you cannot dance Moreška.' Boys begin learning the steps in elementary school. Roles like the kings and the Bula are passed from father to son across generations. The dance is not performed for tourists. It is performed because it has always been performed, because a town that has survived Venetians, Ottomans, Habsburgs, and Yugoslav commissars knows what it means to hold something sacred. Every summer, from May through October, HGD Sveta Cecilija performs 24 times at the open-air Summer Cinema in the old town. The same iron swords. The same archaic Korčulan verse. The same seven battles. Come witness it.

privacyPage.heroSubtitle: How we collect, use, and protect your data

sections.heading: Who We Are
sections.body: HGD Sveta Cecilija (Hrvatsko glazbeno društvo Sveta Cecilija) is a cultural association based in Korčula, Croatia, operating the website moreska.eu. As data controller, we are responsible for your personal data. Contact: info@moreska.eu

sections.heading: Data We Collect and Why
sections.body:
Contact and enquiry forms: We collect your name, email address, and message to respond to your enquiry. Legal basis: legitimate interests.

Performance bookings: We collect your name and email to confirm your booking and deliver your tickets. Legal basis: performance of a contract.

Analytics: With your consent, we use Google Analytics to collect anonymised data about how visitors use this website. No personal data is linked to analytics reports. Legal basis: consent.

sections.heading: Data Retention
sections.body: Contact and enquiry records are retained for 2 years. Booking records are retained for 7 years to meet accounting obligations. Analytics data is retained according to Google's standard settings (26 months).

sections.heading: Your Rights
sections.body: Under GDPR you have the right to access, correct, delete, restrict, or port your personal data, and to withdraw consent at any time. To exercise any of these rights, email us at info@moreska.eu. If you believe your rights have been violated, you may lodge a complaint with the Croatian Personal Data Protection Agency (AZOP) at azop.hr.

sections.heading: Changes to This Policy
sections.body: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. The date of the most recent update appears above. Continued use of the site after any changes constitutes acceptance of the updated policy.

cookiePage.heroSubtitle: What cookies we use and how to manage them

sections.heading: What Are Cookies?
sections.body: Cookies are small text files stored on your device by your browser when you visit a website. They help the site remember information about your visit.

sections.heading: Essential Cookies
sections.body: These cookies are required for the website to function correctly. They do not track you across other sites and cannot be turned off. No personal data is collected by essential cookies.

sections.heading: Analytics Cookies
sections.body: With your consent, we use Google Analytics to understand how visitors use this site. These cookies collect anonymised data: pages visited, time spent, and similar metrics. No personal information is recorded or linked to your identity. You can withdraw consent at any time using the cookie settings banner.

sections.heading: Google Ads & Consent Mode v2
sections.body: With your consent, we use Google Ads conversion tracking to measure the effectiveness of our advertising: specifically, whether visitors who arrive from an ad complete a ticket purchase. This is implemented through the same Google tag used for Analytics; no separate tracker is loaded. We use Google Consent Mode v2: until you accept cookies, all advertising and analytics signals are set to 'denied' by default, and Google receives only anonymised pings (no identifiers, no personalisation). On acceptance, the signals are updated to 'granted' and the purchase event is reported. Declining keeps everything denied. You can withdraw consent at any time by clearing your browser storage for this site.

sections.heading: Meta Pixel
sections.body: With your consent, we use the Meta Pixel (Facebook Pixel) to measure the effectiveness of our advertising on Facebook and Instagram. The pixel records page views and ticket purchases on this site so we can understand which campaigns lead to bookings. Data is shared with Meta Platforms Ireland Ltd. We do not run paid social campaigns without this measurement in place. You can withdraw consent at any time using the cookie settings banner. Learn more: facebook.com/privacy/policy.

sections.heading: Managing Cookies
sections.body: You can control and delete cookies through your browser settings. Deleting or blocking cookies may affect your experience on this site. For analytics cookies, use the consent banner displayed when you first visit. For more on how Google uses data: policies.google.com/privacy.

sections.heading: Changes to This Policy
sections.body: We may update this Cookie Policy from time to time. The date of the most recent update appears above.

refundPage.heroSubtitle: When tickets can and cannot be refunded

sections.heading: Customer Cancellation
sections.body: Tickets are non-refundable if you change your mind or cannot attend. Please buy only once your plans are confirmed. If you bought the wrong performance or made an honest mistake at checkout, email info@moreska.eu and we will help where we reasonably can.

sections.heading: If We Cancel the Performance
sections.body: If HGD Sveta Cecilija cancels a performance entirely, every buyer receives a full refund to the original card. No action is needed on your part: we process these automatically and email you when the refund is sent. Funds typically appear within 5 to 10 business days.

sections.heading: Bad Weather and Venue Changes
sections.body: Our summer performances are held at the Summer Cinema (Ljetno kino). In bad weather a performance may be moved to the indoor Cultural Center Korčula (Centar za kulturu) rather than cancelled. A venue change is not a cancellation and is not an automatic refund trigger: the performance still goes ahead. We notify affected buyers by email with the new venue and a map link. If the new venue genuinely does not work for you, reply to that email and we will review your case individually.

sections.heading: Lost or Stolen Tickets
sections.body: Lost or stolen tickets are not refunded. Your tickets and QR codes are tied to your booking, so you can re-download your PDF tickets at any time from the link in your confirmation email. If you cannot find that email, contact info@moreska.eu and we will resend your tickets.

sections.heading: How Refunds Are Processed
sections.body: All refunds are returned to the original payment card through Stripe, our payment processor. We cannot refund to a different card or by another method. Once we issue a refund, your bank decides exactly when it posts, usually within 5 to 10 business days. You will receive a confirmation email from us when the refund is sent.

sections.heading: Questions and Case-by-Case Requests
sections.body: For anything not covered here, including case-by-case requests after a venue change, email info@moreska.eu. We read every message and aim to reply within two business days.

consent.notice: We'll email your ticket, any changes to the performance, and one short follow-up after the performance. You can opt out at any time.

moreska.heroHeadline: Where Swords Clash and Love Triumphs
moreska.heroSubtitle: Europe's last authentic war dance, performed since 1666.
moreska.lead: Of all the sword dances that once spanned the Mediterranean, from Spain to Sicily, from Venice to the Greek islands, only the Moreška of Korčula has survived intact. Not as a museum piece. As a living performance, performed in the original 17th-century Korčulan dialect, with iron swords that weigh a kilogram each and strike hard enough to produce sparks.
moreska.storyBody: Two kings. One captive maiden. Seven battles to decide her fate. The White King, Osman, arrives to reclaim his betrothed, the Bula, from the Black King, Moro, who has taken her by force. The Bula refuses to yield: "Far more pain for me is your unwanted love than the steel." What follows is the Sfida, the challenge, and then seven kolapi: a sequence of increasingly intense sword-clash figures that escalates from lyrical circling to furious final combat. The Black army is surrounded, their circle shrinking with each figure, until the Black King surrenders his sword at Osman's feet. The Bula is freed. Virtue wins. It has always won.
moreska.danceBody: The Moreška is unique in European folk tradition for its double-sword technique: every dancer wields two iron blades: the right hand strikes, the left parries. The choreography is fixed and has been passed down unchanged for generations. Each of the seven figures has a name: Rugier, Moreška, Finta, Moro in dentro, Križ, Rugier de fuorivia, and the Final Figure, executed at full speed in 4/4 time. The sound alone is worth the journey. Iron on iron, echoing off the stone walls of the old town.
moreska.costumeBody: The moreškant's attire is unlike anything else in Croatian folklore: a Roman-Oriental style tracing back to 17th-century Italian court ballet. The White army wears red; the Black army wears black. Full pleated skirts, white Roman cuffs, sashes, handkerchiefs. Ornate, martial, unmistakable.
moreska.participationBody: Moreška is traditionally performed by males from Korčulan families. Boys begin learning the steps in elementary school. A local saying captures what it means: "You are not a male if you cannot dance Moreška." Roles, including the kings and the Bula, are often passed from father to son across generations.

band.heroHeadline: The Sound of the Sword Dance
band.heroSubtitle: The musical backbone of Moreška since 1937.
band.body1: Since 1937, the Moreška sword dance has breathed to the rhythm of music written especially for it by the renowned Croatian composer Krsto Odak. This is no mere accompaniment to the blades and the footwork: the music was created to grow together with the Moreška, to follow every swing, every meeting of the swords, every moment of tension between the opposing sides. It gives the dancers their tempo, the movement its energy, and the scene a solemn power that makes it feel as though, for an instant, some older age were opening before the viewer.
band.body2: Our orchestra is not bound to the Moreška alone. Throughout the year it gives a series of concerts of its own, chief among them our Christmas concert, the crowning event of the season. The orchestra brings together musicians of every age, from schoolchildren to retirees, proof that music connects people regardless of their years and builds a togetherness that reaches across generations.

klapa.heroSubtitle: Dalmatian a cappella, UNESCO intangible cultural heritage.
klapa.body1: Before the swords clash, voices rise. Klapa singing is woven into the cultural identity of Korčula, an island known for its rich musical tradition. Voices in harmony without instruments, emotion carried through song, a sense of belonging. Klapa is one of the most recognisable symbols of Dalmatia, recognised by UNESCO as intangible cultural heritage and safeguarded for future generations.
klapa.body2: Mixed klapa Sveta Cecilija was founded in 2016, and to this day its many-voiced singing sets the stage for the Moreška that follows, deepening the experience of Korčulan tradition.

choir.body1: Reborn in 2023, our choir carries on the rich tradition of the first singing society in Dalmatia, a tradition we proudly revived back in 1991. Bringing together music lovers from across the generations, the choir safeguards a musical heritage and, at the same time, gives it new life through singing and performing together.
choir.body2: Its repertoire draws above all on home-grown music, from arrangements of popular songs to Dalmatian and klapa pieces that hold especially close the Mediterranean spirit and the sense of togetherness. With every rehearsal and every performance the choir grows, and year after year it becomes more assured, more closely knit, and more accomplished, proof that dedication, fellowship, and a love of song bring results.
choir.body3: Beyond the music itself, the choir plays an important social role: it brings people together, nurtures friendships, and passes a love of shared singing on to younger generations, creating a space where tradition stays alive yet always open to new voices and new interpretations.
